data.title: Irish Consumer Confidence Falls in July
data.description:
Ireland’s Credit Union Consumer Sentiment Index dropped to 59.1 in July 2025 from 62.5 in June, hitting a three-month low as households grew more concerned about the rising cost of living and the impact of tariffs on EU exports to the US. Survey components related to household finances and spending saw the steepest declines.

Analysts attributed the weakness to persistent inflation concerns and signals from the government that fewer support measures may be included in the upcoming October budget.

Trade uncertainty also played a role, with the looming threat of a 30% US tariff on EU exports dampening sentiment.

Still, hopes remain that a pending US-EU trade deal, reportedly set to impose a reduced 15% tariff on most goods, could help ease those fears.

This decline in confidence comes despite continued strength in the domestic economy, including solid tax revenues and ongoing job growth.

data.title: OFX Gets New Bull as Platform Rollout Progresses — Market Talk
data.description: OFX gets a new bull at Canaccord Genuity, where analyst Owen Humphries is increasingly confident in the rollout of the international payments provider's new client platform. Raising his recommendation to buy from hold, Humphries tells clients in a note that the migration of existing clients to the platform is accelerating and that all Australian corporate customers will be there by the end of the September quarter. Foreign-exchange activity is higher among migrated clients and retention is improving, he adds. Canaccord raises its target price 10% to A$1.10. Shares are up 0.6% at A$0.845. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Japanese Stocks Decline on Profit-Taking
data.description:
The Nikkei 225 fell 0.4% to below 41,700, while the Topix Index dropped 0.6% to 2,960 on Friday, as investors booked profits following a sharp rally that pushed both indexes to new highs.

Despite the pullback, both benchmarks remain on track to post weekly gains of over 4%, buoyed by optimism around the recently signed US-Japan trade deal.

Under the agreement, Japanese exports to the US will face a 15% tariff, well below the 25% threatened by President Trump earlier this month, easing some trade-related uncertainty.

On the economic front, Tokyo’s core inflation came in slightly below expectations for July but remained well above the Bank of Japan’s 2% target, reinforcing expectations for another rate hike later this year.

In corporate news, Shin-Etsu Chemical plunged nearly 10% after issuing a weaker profit outlook for fiscal 2025.

Other notable losers included Lasertec (-1.5%), Toyota Motor (-1.7%), and Keyence (-1.7%).

data.title: Oil prices climb on US-EU trade optimism, Russian gasoline cuts
data.description:
Oil prices rose on Friday, buoyed by optimism over a potential trade deal between the U.S. and the European Union and reports of Russian plans to restrict gasoline exports to most countries.

Brent crude futures gained 17 cents, or 0.3%, to $69.35 a barrel by 0027 GMT. U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures climbed 15 cents, or 0.2%, to $66.18 per barrel.

Oil settled 1% higher on Thursday, driven by media reports of expected cuts to Russian gasoline exports. This overshadowed news of Chevron Corp potentially securing U.S. approval to resume production in Venezuela.

President Donald Trump's administration is preparing to allow limited oil operations in the sanctioned OPEC nation, the Wall Street Journal reported.

U.S. crude inventory draws and hopes for a trade deal between the U.S. and the EU for lower tariffs were lifting futures, which fell earlier in the week over fears of a worsening global trade war.

"I am encouraged by the way crude oil held and bounced away from the $65/64 support band this week, which keeps hopes intact of a rebound back towards $70," said Tony Sycamore, an analyst at IG.

U.S. Energy Information Administration data on Wednesday showed crude inventories fell last week by 3.2 million barrels to 419 million barrels, far exceeding analysts' expectations in a Reuters poll for a 1.6 million-barrel draw.

Two European diplomats said on Wednesday that the EU and the U.S. were moving toward a trade deal that could include a 15% U.S. baseline tariff on EU imports and possible exemptions. That could pave the way for another major trade agreement following a deal with Japan.

Investors will also be turning their focus to upcoming economic data next week from the world's top two economies and largest oil consumer - factory activity data from China and key U.S. indicators such as inflation, jobs and inventory data.

"It is a big week next week data-wise," IG's Sycamore said.

data.title: Gold ticks up on softer US dollar, heads for weekly gain
data.description:
Gold prices inched higher in early Asian trading on Friday, buoyed by an overall weaker dollar, although signs of progress in trade negotiations between the U.S. and its trading partners kept gains in check.

FUNDAMENTALS

* Spot gold was up 0.1% at $3,371.86 per ounce, as of 0051 GMT. Bullion is up 0.6% this week. U.S. gold futures were little changed at $3,374.80.

* The U.S. dollar index was headed for its worst week in a month, making greenback-priced gold less expensive for other currency holders.

* The European Union and United States now appear to be heading towards a possible trade deal, according to EU diplomats, which would result in a broad 15% tariff on EU goods imported into the U.S., mirroring a framework agreement Washington struck with Japan.

* The S&P 500 and the Nasdaq notched record closing highs overnight as signs of easing global trade tensions lifted risk sentiment among investors.

* Data showed U.S. jobless claims unexpectedly fell last week, signalling a steady labour market despite sluggish hiring making it harder for the unemployed to find work.

* The European Central Bank left interest rates unchanged on Thursday, pausing after seven straight cuts, and offered a modestly upbeat assessment of the euro zone economy.

* The Federal Reserve is also widely expected to leave rates unchanged at its July 29–30 meeting, but markets continue to price in a potential rate cut in September. (FEDWATCH)

* SPDR Gold Trust , the world's largest gold-backed exchange-traded fund, said its holdings rose 0.24% to 957.09 metric tons on Thursday from 954.80 tons on Wednesday.

* Spot silver was flat at $39.10 per ounce, platinum gained 0.4% to $1,413.55 and palladium climbed 0.8% to $1,237.35.

DATA/EVENTS (GMT)

0500

Japan Leading Indicator Revised May

0600

UK Retail Sales MM, YY June

0600

0800

Germany Ifo Business Climate, Curr Conditions, Expectations New July

data.title: Plenti Bulls See Opportunity From Battery Subsidy — Market Talk
data.description: Plenti's bulls at Wilsons see the credit provider getting plenty of opportunities from Australia's home-battery subsidy program. Wilsons analysts point out that Plenti has a 24% share of renewable loan originations, indicating strong favorable exposure to the federal government's A$2.3 billion battery scheme. Many battery installers have said that July 2025 demand is about four times higher than a year earlier, the analysts add. With growth also strong in automotive and personal segments, Wilsons sees Plenti well positioned to deliver cash profit growth. Wilsons raises its target price 9.1% to A$1.44 and stays overweight on the stock, which is down 0.5% at A$0.945. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: ASX 200 Extends Losses, but Still Set for Weekly Gain
data.description:
The S&P/ASX 200 dropped 0.5% to 8,664 in morning trade on Friday, extending losses from the previous session, with nearly all sectors in the red.

The downturn was mainly driven by declines in materials, financials, and real estate.

Tech stocks also edged lower, despite the Nasdaq on Wall Street closing at fresh record highs overnight, supported by strong earnings from Alphabet.

Traders continued to monitor developments in US trade talks with key partners, as well as upcoming negotiations with China in Stockholm next week.

Caution also prevailed ahead of the release of Australia’s monthly and quarterly inflation data next week, which could guide the RBA’s monetary policy path.

Among the worst performers were Champion Iron (–4.8%), Liontown Resources (–3.7%), Cettire (–2.9%), and Bellevue Gold (–2.8%).

For the week, the index is on track for a 0.3% gain, which would mark its second straight weekly advance.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Markets Headlines at 9 PM ET: S&P 500, Nasdaq Composite Extend Record Run | Trump ...
data.description:
S&P 500, Nasdaq Composite Extend Record Run

A mixed bag of earnings and economic data still left the indexes at all-time highs.

----

Trump Administration Greenlights Chevron to Resume Pumping Oil in Venezuela

The decision marks a softening in the White House's approach to the South American country.

----

Canada Retail Sales Look to Rebound After 1.1% Drop in May

There are early signs Canada's economy firmed coming into the summer, with advance tallies pointing to the strongest retail sales growth since the end of last year and the first rise in manufacturing sales in five months.

----

Fewer U.S. Jobless Claims Were Filed Last Week

U.S. initial jobless claims declined last week, according to the Department of Labor, extending a stretch of evidence that layoffs have remained constrained.

----

ECB Holds Rates Steady as U.S. Tariff Negotiations Cloud Outlook

The central bank left its key deposit rate at 2% after making eight cuts since last June.

----

Turkey Central Bank Returns to Rate Cuts Path After Unrest Forced Hike

The central bank said it would lower its benchmark rate to 43% from 46%, restarting an easing cycle that had been disrupted by a political furor.

----

Trump Says He Won't Fire Powell Despite Bout of Bickering Over Renovation Costs

The president turned a rare visit to the U.S. central bank into political theater that included hard hats and a back slap.

----

Global Economies Show Resilience Despite Tariff Fears

The U.S. economy and its peers in Europe and Asia continued to show resilience in the face of high levels of uncertainty about the future of trade and international relations, although export orders weakened.

----

Beijing's Trade Tactics, Ukraine Stance Cast Pall Over Europe-China Ties

After Trump's election, China and Europe sought warmer ties, but rifts over Russia and trade sparked conflict.

----

Asia-U.S. Trade Deals Pick Up as Tariff Deadline Nears

Excluding a truce with China, four Asian nations have now reached deals, with just over a week before the grace period expires.

----

Chinese Social-Media Hashtag Signals Growing Frustration With Job Market

The phrase "beauty in the time of economic upturns" has been trending on Chinese sites for months, a nostalgic reference to the 2000s-2010s.

----

Trump's New Trade Standard Takes Shape With 15% Tariff Deal

A deal with Japan and a potential deal with Europe follow months of uncertainty and will likely raise prices while offering a bit of clarity for global trade.

----

South Korea's Economy Rebounds in Second Quarter

The pickup in Asia's fourth-largest economy was largely driven by resilient exports and policy support.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Company Headlines at 9 PM ET: FCC Approves Paramount's $8 Billion Merger With Skydance | Intel ...
data.description:
FCC Approves Paramount's $8 Billion Merger With Skydance

The signoff on the deal, which could close next month, follows Paramount's settlement of a Trump lawsuit.

----

Intel to Lay Off 15% of Workers, Cancel Billions in Projects in Bid for Rebound

The American chip-making giant is facing a strategic crossroads as it chases rivals like Nvidia and TSMC in the global silicon boom.

----

Pinnacle Financial, Synovus Financial Agree to $8.6 Billion Merger

The merged regional bank will focus on the fastest-growth markets in the Southeast. Shares of both companies fell in after-hours trading.

----

KKR Faces EU Probe Into Information Provided in $26 Billion NetCo Acquisition

The European Union opened an investigation into whether KKR supplied the bloc's merger regulator with false or misleading information.

----

Newmont Second-Quarter Earnings Rise as Gold Prices Surge

The mining company reported higher profit as concerns about market volatility have driven the price of gold to record highs in recent months.

----

Deckers First-Quarter Results Fly on Higher Hoka, Ugg Sales

The footwear and apparel company's financial results jumped as Hoka and Ugg shoes boosted sales.

----

Puma Cuts Guidance Amid Sales Decline, Tariff Pressures

The sportswear manufacturer said it now expects sales to decline a low double-digit percentage over the year, compared to its previous guidance for growth.

----

Elon Musk Tells Tesla Investors to Focus on a Future Filled With Robots

Company points out early progress on robotaxis and bots, as revenue from electric vehicles plummets.

----

Weyerhaeuser Reports Lower Profit, Sales in Second Quarter

The lumber producer reported lower profit and sales, though its top executive noted the company will continue to return cash back to shareholders during a challenging market backdrop.

----

Boston Beer Expects More Moderate Tariff Effects as Profit Rises

The brewer, which owns the Samuel Adams and Truly brands, estimated that tariffs will pose an unfavorable effect on costs of approximately $15 million to $20 million for the full year.

----

Midmarket Firm Altamont Scores Big Win in Accelerant IPO

Todd Boehly's Eldridge and Barings also sold shares in the initial public offering that valued the insurance technology company at some $6 billion in its first day of trading.

----

Private Credit and Wealth Strategies Boost Blackstone's Growth

The private-market investment firm plans to create new products for individual investors with an eye on retirement plans.

----

Teck Green Lights Extension on Big Canadian Copper Mine

The miner expects to invest up to $1.8 billion in the Highland Valley mine, which would represent the largest critical minerals investment in British Columbia's history.

----

Charles Schwab Authorizes New $20 Billion Stock Repurchase Plan, Shares Touch All-Time High

The company's co-chairman said the authorization reflects Schwab's sustained business and financial momentum and continued confidence in the long-term prospects for the firm.

----

Trump Administration Greenlights Chevron to Resume Pumping Oil in Venezuela

The decision marks a softening in the White House's approach to the South American country.

----

Dow Inc. Cuts Dividend by 50% and the Stock Sinks

Dow cuts its quarterly dividend to 35 cents a share from 70 cents.

----

UnitedHealth Confirms Civil and Criminal Justice Department Probes

The healthcare conglomerate said it has now begun complying with formal criminal and civil requests from the Justice Department.

----

Union Pacific, Norfolk Southern Say They Are in Advanced Talks Over Railroad Merger

A combination could create the largest rail operator in the country with a sprawling rail network spanning the continent.

data.title: CARsgen: Commercializing Cancer Therapy in China Supported Results >2171.HK
South Korea Stocks Set to End Week on Muted Note
data.description:
The KOSPI Index edged lower in a volatile session on Friday morning, hovering around 3,187 after two days of gains, as sentiment was rattled by uncertainty over U.S.-South Korea trade talks.

Negotiations scheduled for this week were postponed due to a scheduling conflict involving U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent.

On Wall Street, stocks ended mixed overnight, despite record closes for the S&P 500 and Nasdaq, buoyed by signs of progress toward a U.S.-EU trade deal.

Among notable laggards in Seoul were LG Energy (-1.8%), Celltrion (-1.6%), Samsung Biologics (-0.9%), and Korea Electric Power (-0.5%).

For the week, the KOSPI is on track to end largely flat, with investors awaiting a third round of U.S.-China trade talks in Stockholm, aimed at extending a tariff truce set to expire on August 12.

Also in focus is next week’s U.S. Fed policy meeting, amid renewed pressure from President Trump to cut rates following his visit to the central bank on Thursday.

data.title: Nestle (Malaysia) May See Gradual Recovery — Market Talk
data.description: Nestle (Malaysia)'s brand image seems to be gradually recovering from the impact of earlier consumer boycotts, and sales volume is expected to stabilize, says Maybank IB analyst Jade Tam in a note. The company is managing raw material cost pressures through internal pricing strategies, she says. Recent government stimulus and greater clarity on fuel-subsidy reforms could support demand for consumer staples, as pressure on household spending eases slightly, she adds. Maybank maintains a buy rating on Nestle (Malaysia) and keeps its target price at MYR96.70. Shares are flat at MYR82.30. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2107 ET - Nestle (Malaysia)'s brand image seems to be gradually recovering from the impact of earlier consumer boycotts, and sales volume is expected to stabilize, says Maybank IB analyst Jade Tam in a note. The company is managing raw material cost pressures through internal pricing strategies, she says. Recent government stimulus and greater clarity on fuel-subsidy reforms could support demand for consumer staples, as pressure on household spending eases slightly, she adds. Maybank maintains a buy rating on Nestle (Malaysia) and keeps its target price at MYR96.70. Shares are flat at MYR82.30. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2057 ET - Macquarie's first-quarter update leaves Citi analyst Thomas Strong with a sense of déjà vu. He thinks that the Australian financial giant's June-quarter earnings are soft compared with consensus forecasts, but doesn't see any reason for full-year expectations to shift materially given management's comments on the short-term outlook. The upshot is that investors are facing a familiar earnings skew toward the second half of its fiscal year, he tells clients in a note. With asset realizations a key factor in the first-quarter miss, Strong thinks that management's confidence suggests greater visibility through the remainder of fiscal 2026. Citi has a neutral rating and A$200.00 target price on the stock, which is up 0.3% at A$214.48. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2052 ET - Whitehaven's 4Q run-of-mine output beats consensus expectations and translates to strong sales, Jefferies analyst Daniel Roden says in a note. The miner's realized price is 5% lower than analysts anticipated, but offset by a strong result on costs, says Roden. Net debt is also better than expected. Roden says Whitehaven's balance sheet looks resilient at a time when coal prices have been weak. "Good operational control and an improving coal market outlook enhance expectations of deleveraging and capital returns," he says. Jefferies has a buy rating and A$6.40 target on Whitehaven. The stock is down 0.1% at A$7.02, outperforming Australia's materials index, which is sharply lower.(rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2045 ET - Treasury Wine Estates' aspiration for modest earnings growth from its luxury business in the Americas looks to be out of reach, according to Jefferies analysts. They retain a buy rating on the Australian producer, but reckon that the impact of its forced distributor switch in California will hit fiscal 2026 earnings. They now forecast a 10% decline in Americas luxury earnings for the period. The analysts tell clients in a note that they expect a hit to near-term shipments, with new distributor Breakthru likely to ramp up capability over time. Jefferies lowers its target price by 13% to A$10.00. Shares are down 1.2% at A$7.98. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2034 ET - Bapcor's CEO retains the confidence of Jefferies analysts despite their concern over the largely unexplained exit of three directors from the auto-parts supplier's board. They tell clients in a note that Angus McKay is the right leader for the Australia-listed business, which has now downgraded earnings expectations five times in two years. They acknowledge the difficulty of turning around the business and the extent of the uncertainty over the outlook, but stress that they still see long-term opportunity. The stock looks very cheap to them at 13 times their FY 2026 earnings forecast. Jefferies cuts target price 17% to A$4.90 but keeps a buy rating on the stock, which is down 0.55% at A$3.64. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2009 ET - Japanese stocks are lower as initial enthusiasm over the U.S. trade deal subsides following two days of sharp gains. Carmakers are leading declines. Subaru is down 3.3% and Mazda Motor is 3.3% lower. USD/JPY is at 147.22, compared with 146.22 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching corporate earnings, with Fanuc scheduled to announce its quarterly results later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 0.3% at 41691.09. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2000 ET - Whitehaven recorded a good cost performance for FY25, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart, after what he calls a "robust" 4Q result from the Australian coal miner. McTaggart says Whitehaven's 4Q saleable production was 9% higher than Citi expected. Cost guidance for FY26 will come next month with Whitehaven's FY fiscal results, he notes. Citi has a buy rating on the stock, with a target price of A$7.00. Whitehaven ended Thursday at A$7.03/share. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1947 ET - Japanese stocks may decline as initial enthusiasm over the U.S. trade deal subsides following two days of sharp gains. Nikkei futures are down 0.5% at 41625 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.10, compared with 146.22 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on corporate earnings, with Fanuc set to report its results later in the day. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 1.6% to 41826.34 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1910 ET [Dow Jones]--Australia's S&P/ASX 200 seems on course for a weekly decline, with local stock futures pointing to a lower open for the benchmark index. ASX futures are down by almost 0.5% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the ASX 200 will add to Thursday's 0.3% slip. It is down almost 0.6% so far this week. Ahead of the open, Whitehaven Coal said annual production and sales were at the top end of guidance, with costs and capex likely lower than estimated. U.S. stocks were mixed. The S&P 500 added less than 0.1% for its fourth consecutive record, its longest such streak since December. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite gained 0.2%, also hitting a record. The DJIA slipped 0.7%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1859 ET - Boston Beer expects its new Sun Cruiser brand to continue to boost sales in its non-beer category. The vodka iced tea, which launched last summer, continues to grow volumes week-over-week as distribution expands to larger chain retailers, the company says during a call with analysts. "We believe Sun Cruiser will be the next iconic brand for the company and an important growth contributor for the 'beyond beer' category," CEO Michael Spillane says. The drink has been the company's fastest ramping product so far, he says, adding that the company plans to continue to invest in it heavily. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

1747 ET - Deckers Outdoor has started raising prices on some of its shoes by around $5. The company started implementing price hikes on July 1 to offset the cost of tariffs and is planning to add more increases throughout the year. "We've been selective and staggered in our approach," CEO Stefano Caroti told analysts during a call, adding that the company doesn't want to raise prices for kids' shoes. Management is also considering hiking prices for not-yet-launched models, as well as existing ones. Caroti said the company would continue monitoring tariffs and altering its pricing plan as the situation evolves. Shares rise 8.7% after the bell. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1731 ET - Deckers Outdoors shares settled slightly after a significant 1Q earnings beat gave investors an initial thrill. Executive commentary about tariffs and persistent uncertainty in the U.S. dampened the stock's jump after the bell to around 7%, after it was up 16% at one point. The shoemaker again declined to share full-year guidance because there is still macroeconomic uncertainty. It upped its unmitigated tariff estimate to account for potentially higher duties on Vietnamese imports. More sluggish demand in the U.S. — around 3% sales growth versus 50% internationally — offset some of Deckers' gains in direct-to-consumer sales. (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

data.title: Australian energy stocks gain most in three weeks as oil prices climb
data.description:
** Aussie energy stocks rise as much as 1.3% to 9,248.90, their biggest intraday pct gain since July 3

** Stock set for second straight weekly gain, if current momentum holds

** Oil prices rose overnight on news of U.S. crude draws and expected cuts to Russian gasoline exports

** Australia's top independent gas producer Woodside Energy rises 2.1% and Santos up 0.8%

** YTD, AXEJ up 7.1%
